title: 1.0 What is Arduino?
content:
Arduino is an open-source development platform created to allow anyone interested in programming electronics to develop software and hardware applications. It consists of various microcontroller boards commonly used by beginners, university students, and hobbyists to create interactive electronic devices.

The Arduino system includes a programmable circuit board capable of interpreting digital input signals and generating output actions. Arduino boards receive inputs through sensors or buttons and control output devices using built-in instructions or external programs written by the user.

First and foremost, Arduino simplifies the process of connecting electronics to your computer because you do not need to know much about programming to build devices. Users only need to connect the microcontroller to a computer and upload a script for the invention to work.

One advantage of Arduino is that it is open-source, meaning its hardware and software architecture is available to the public. Anyone who wants to learn and use existing technologies can do so without any fees.

Some basic Arduino devices include the following:

- Home automation system
- Robot controlled from a smartphone application
- Automatic weather station

Arduino therefore connects the digital world with the real world and serves as an excellent educational technology.

title: 1.1 History and Uses of Arduino
content:
Arduino was invented in 2005 in Italy by several university professors and students. They focused on developing an affordable and easy-to-use device for teaching electronics and programming.

Before the invention of Arduino, working with microcontrollers required expensive equipment and specialized coding skills. With Arduino, these processes became much more accessible.

Over time, Arduino became very popular worldwide. Today it is used for:

- Educational purposes in schools and universities
- DIY projects (Do It Yourself)
- Product prototyping
- IoT (Internet of Things)
- Simpler industrial automation

The Arduino community became one of the main advantages of Arduino technology. Many Arduino owners share their experiences on forums, and millions of tutorials are available online. Therefore, finding solutions to almost any Arduino-related problem is relatively easy.

Besides being a product, Arduino also inspired other companies and played an important role in the development of the maker movement.

title: 1.2 Arduino Boards
content:
Arduino boards are hardware devices used to run programs that you write. There are many different Arduino board models. Each board is specialized for specific tasks. Among the commonly used Arduino boards, we can highlight the following:

- Arduino Uno: the most popular board, suitable for beginners. Used in the Hardware and Networks laboratory classes in the second year of IT studies.
- Arduino Nano: small size, suitable for compact projects.
- Arduino Mega: a board that provides more I/O pins and memory than other boards.
- Arduino Due: a more advanced board that uses a different microcontroller architecture.
- Arduino Leonardo: a board that can be used as a keyboard or mouse.

Every Arduino board contains the following components:

- Microcontroller
- Digital I/O pins
- Analog input pins
- Power supply connections
- USB connector

When choosing a suitable Arduino board, it is important to understand what requirements the board must fulfill. For general use, Arduino Uno is always recommended.

title: 1.3 Arduino IDE
content:
Arduino IDE is software used to create and upload code to Arduino hardware. It is user-friendly and suitable for all experience levels while still being effective enough for advanced users. The IDE uses a programming language derived from C/C++, although it differs in its own ways.

All Arduino programs include two basic and main functions:

- void setup(): This function runs at the start of the project. It is used, for example, to initialize serial communication or configure pins.
- void loop(): This function runs repeatedly.

Arduino IDE also contains:

- Code editor
- Compiler
- Serial monitor
- External libraries (e.g. LiquidCrystalI2C)

Compiling code in Arduino IDE is very simple. It works in 4 steps:

- Connect the Arduino board to the PC using a USB connector.
- Select the correct board in Arduino IDE.
- Program your application.
- Select the 'Upload' option (however, there is also the 'Verify' option, which checks the functionality of the code before uploading it to the board).

One major disadvantage of Arduino IDE is that compared to more advanced IDEs (e.g. Visual Studio Code), it does not clearly indicate where an error is in the code. Your code may be completely wrong, and Arduino IDE might not warn you. You only discover the error when trying to upload or verify the program. On the other hand, Arduino IDE has the advantage that within seconds your program can start running on the board. Of course, it depends on the size and complexity of the code, but generally Arduino code uploads very quickly.

title: 2.0 Voltage, Current and Resistance
content:
We all know or have heard these terms: voltage, resistance, and current. These three quantities form the foundation of how electricity behaves in a circuit. Anyone working in electronics, whether building simple circuits or developing more complex systems such as microcontroller-based projects, must thoroughly understand these concepts.

Voltage is the difference in electrical potential between two points in a circuit. It is represented by the letter V, although you may come across the letter U being used. Voltage is surprisingly... measured in volts [V]. Think of it as pressure in a water system. Just as water pressure pushes water through pipes, voltage pushes electrical charges through a conductor. Voltage is usually supplied by sources such as batteries, power adapters, or generators. Different devices have different voltage requirements, and using the wrong voltage may damage components.

Current is the rate of flow of electrical charge through a conductor. It is represented by the letter I and measured in amperes [A]. Applying voltage to a circuit causes electrons to move, which creates current. The strength of the current is determined by the voltage and resistance in the circuit.

Resistance, as the name suggests, is the opposition to the flow of electric current. It is represented by the letter R and measured in ohms [Ω]. All materials have some resistance, but with resistors we can deliberately regulate resistance. High resistance means less current flows, while low resistance means more current can pass through. Resistance is essential for protecting sensitive components, controlling LED brightness, and maintaining circuit safety.

A useful analogy for visualizing all three concepts is to imagine electricity as water flowing through pipes.
- voltage: water pressure
- current: water flow speed
- resistance: pipe size

If you understand how these three factors work together, you can predict how a circuit will behave and avoid common mistakes such as short circuits or overheating components.

title: 2.1 Ohm's Law
content:
Arguably the simplest, most well-known, and most essential principle of electronics is Ohm's Law, because it directly relates voltage, current, and resistance. It provides a simple mathematical relationship that can be used to analyze and design circuits. The law is expressed by the formula:

V = I * R

This equation can also be rearranged depending on what we want to calculate:

I = V/R
R = V/I

Ohm's Law states that increasing voltage while keeping resistance constant increases current, and increasing resistance while keeping voltage constant decreases current. This relationship is very important in circuit design. For example, you must use a resistor to limit current when working with an LED. Otherwise, too much current could flow through it and permanently damage the LED.

Ohm's Law is commonly used to identify and solve problems. If a circuit does not behave as expected, measuring voltage and current can reveal whether resistance is too high or too low, or whether there is a fault such as a broken connection.

At more advanced levels, Ohm's Law is also used in the context of understanding power consumption and efficiency. It is often combined with the power formula:

P = V * I

This is useful for determining how much power a component consumes. In short, knowing how to use Ohm's Law means you can create circuits that work properly and safely, making it a fundamental principle of electrical engineering and electronics.

title: 2.2 Basic Components
content:
Electronic circuits contain many components. Each one plays a role and serves a specific purpose. It is important to learn these basic components to understand how circuits work and to be able to create your own projects. Here are some common components that you will definitely encounter during Hardware and Networks laboratory classes:

A resistor is used to limit or control the flow of current in a circuit. It ensures that components receive the correct amount of current and prevents damage. Resistors come in different values, usually marked with colored bands.

A capacitor is a device that temporarily stores electrical energy and releases it when needed. It is widely used to smooth power signals, filter noise, and for timing applications. There are several different types of capacitors, such as ceramic and electrolytic capacitors, each suitable for different purposes.

An LED (Light Emitting Diode) is a special type of diode that emits light when current passes through it. LEDs are energy-efficient and are commonly used for indicators, displays, and lighting. They require a resistor to limit current.

A potentiometer is a type of variable resistor that can be manually adjusted, usually by turning a knob or sliding a control. It is often used to control things such as volume, brightness, or sensor thresholds. In a circuit, it can be used as a voltage divider that provides a variable output voltage depending on its position.

A button is a basic input device that temporarily opens or closes a circuit when pressed. It is commonly used to trigger actions such as turning devices on or off, or sending signals to microcontrollers. Buttons usually require additional components (e.g. resistors) to behave stably and predictably.

Of course, there are many other components in the world of electronics (e.g. LCD displays), but these components form the fundamental foundation of common Arduino projects created in school.

title: 3.0 UART
content:
UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver Transmitter) is one of the most common methods of serial communication. As the name suggests, it is an asynchronous communication protocol that allows two devices to exchange data without requiring a shared clock signal. UART is very commonly used in Arduino boards, computers, GPS modules, Bluetooth/WiFi modules, and many other devices.

UART communication works using two main wires. One wire is used for transmitting data and is called transmitter - TX. The second wire is used for receiving data and is called receiver - RX. Therefore, UART is full-duplex because it contains two communication channels.

Communication between devices must be crossed. This means the transmitter of one device is connected to the receiver of the other device and vice versa. Both devices must also share a common ground wire (GND).

UART is asynchronous communication. Devices do not use a separate clock signal to synchronize data transfer. Instead, both devices must agree in advance on the same transmission speed. This speed is known as the baud rate. Common baud rates include 9600, 57600, or 115200 bps. If both devices are not configured to the same speed, data will be received incorrectly.

During UART communication, data is transmitted bit by bit. Each transmitted character contains a start bit, data bits, and one or more stop bits. The start bit signals the beginning of transmission and the stop bit signals the end of the data frame. Sometimes an additional parity bit is included for error checking.

One of the key strengths of UART communication is its simplicity. The implementation process is fairly straightforward, and most microcontrollers already contain built-in UART hardware. UART is best suited for communication between two devices, such as between an Arduino device and a computer. UART is also frequently used for debugging applications through the serial monitor.

The main disadvantage of UART communication is its inability to support many devices simultaneously. UART is primarily designed for communication between two devices. Another weakness of UART is its relatively low speed compared to faster communication methods such as SPI protocols or newer technologies. UART communication is strongly recommended for simple communication tasks, text data transfer, application debugging, and communication with modules where speed is not critical.
